root_cause: The web service was deployed with RELEASE=v2, a bad release that fails /healthz readiness checks (503) and returns HTTP 500 on /orders (checkout). The previous good release was v1; payments was still on v1 and healthy.
actions_taken: Recreated the sreft-web container with RELEASE=v1 (same image deploy-svc:local, same network alias web, port mapping 8083:8080, restart policy). Verified /healthz returns 200 and /orders returns {"ok":true,"release":"v1"} via the service network and host port.
postmortem: A bad deploy of web (RELEASE=v2) introduced a regression that failed readiness and broke checkout (/orders → 500) while the container stayed up and the DB/payments path remained fine. Detection was straightforward from container logs and env (RELEASE=v2 vs payments on v1). Rollback was a simple env change to RELEASE=v1 and container recreate—no data loss. Follow-ups: gate releases with automated smoke tests on /healthz and /orders before promoting; use progressive delivery (canary/blue-green) so a bad RELEASE cannot take all traffic; keep RELEASE/version visible in health responses and dashboards; document a one-command rollback for this compose stack. Blameless: the failure mode was intentional in the bad release and would have been caught by pre-prod checks or canaries.
